note: Sumerian-Babylonian sacred order normalized onto the 21-agent spine. Primary array: 50 me, the divine decrees that Enki gave to Inanna. Secondary: the Anunnaki, the great gods, with the seven who decree fate (An, Enlil, Enki, Ninhursag, Nanna, Utu, Inanna) flagged. Babylonian theology counts 50 great gods, 7 deciders of fate, 300 heavenly Igigi and 600 Anunnaki of the netherworld - the number 50 also echoes Enlil's epithet 'lord of the fifty me'.
me_note: The me (Sumerian me, divine decrees and ontological powers of civilization) appear in several partly fragmentary lists in 'Inana and Enki' (ETCSL 1.3.1), where Enki bestows them on Inanna; scholars count roughly 60-100 entries. This is a normalized, representative set of 50 well-attested me. Offices, virtues and vices alike are listed, since the me encompass the whole of civilization, both its order and its disorder.
mera_axis: 9-dimensional axis (mera 1-9): chakras 1-7 (Muladhara..Sahasrara) + Monada (8) + Absolut (9); each spine agent carries its mera.

anunnaki.name: An
anunnaki.domain: sky, heaven, supreme father of the gods

anunnaki.name: Enlil
anunnaki.domain: air, wind, storm, kingship, the Tablets of Destiny

anunnaki.name: Enki
anunnaki.domain: fresh water of the abzu, wisdom, magic, crafts, keeper of the me

anunnaki.name: Ninhursag
anunnaki.domain: earth, mountains, the mother of life and birth

anunnaki.name: Nanna
anunnaki.domain: the moon, the monthly cycles, cattle

anunnaki.name: Inanna
anunnaki.domain: love, war, the planet Venus, sovereignty

anunnaki.name: Ereshkigal
anunnaki.domain: queen of the underworld, the dead

anunnaki.name: Nergal
anunnaki.domain: war, plague, scorching death, the underworld

anunnaki.name: Ninurta
anunnaki.domain: war, the hunt, agriculture, champion of the gods

anunnaki.name: Dumuzi
anunnaki.domain: shepherd, vegetation, fertility, the dying-and-rising consort of Inanna

svet_ra.note: Godship and Kingship - the divine sovereignty descending from An, source of all decree.

iskra.note: The kurgarra cult-performer and treachery - the liminal, shape-shifting spark.

brahma.note: The high en-priesthood and the nindingir priestess - the creative sacred office.

vishnu.note: The enduring crown, shepherdship and the exalted throne - the preservation of rule.

ananda.note: The holy tavern and the art of song - the bliss of feast and music.

shanti.note: Attentiveness, the soothing of the heart and elderhood - the offices of calm.

jnana.note: Truth, wisdom and the decreeing of fate - Enki's discerning intellect.

shiva.note: Descent into the underworld and the dagger and sword - dissolution and the blade.

tejas.note: Ascent from the underworld and the battle standard - the radiant return and rallying light.

sarasvati.note: The isib incantation-priest, counsel, forthright speech and the scribe - the powers of the word.

akasha.note: The holy shrine (nigin-gar) - consecrated sacred space.

prema.note: The egizi priestess and the art of lovemaking - the powers of devotion and union.

parvati.note: The shepherd's staff and crook and the reed-worker's craft - nurture and the domestic hand.

vayu.note: Slanderous speech and the resounding instrument - words and sound carried on the air.

shakti.note: The noble scepter, the sacred cult prostitute, heroism and the quiver - Inanna's fierce power of love and war.

agni.note: Power and might and the metalworker's craft - the smelting fire and raw force.

varuna.note: The gudug libation-priest and the art of lamentation - water poured out and grief remembered.

lakshmi.note: Procreation and the leather-worker's craft - fertility and the goods of plenty.

prithvi.note: The carpenter, the smith and the builder - the crafts that raise the solid world.

dharma.note: The throne of kingship, the measuring rod and line, and judgment - lawful order and measure.

karma.note: The plundering of cities and the rebellious land - the strife that returns as consequence.

items.me: The holy measuring rod and line

sources: S. N. Kramer & D. Wolkstein - Inanna: Queen of Heaven and Earth (1983)
oracc.museum.upenn.edu/amgg - Ancient Mesopotamian Gods and Goddesses
